coredata.dc:title: Concentration-dependent emissive lignin-derived graphene quantum dots for bioimaging and anti-counterfeiting
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Ruibin
Guo, Ziyu
Liu, Yuqian
Jiao, Liang
Xiao, Tianhua
Ji, Hairui
Qin, Yanlin
Hua, Feiguo
Dai, Hongqi
Min, Yonggang
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) derived from lignin, which is the most naturally abundant aromatic biopolymer on earth, have found promising application in many fields. Here, we demonstrate that GQDs with concentration-dependent photoluminescence emission can be produced from lignin by employing a non-oxidative method. In brief, o-aminobenzenesulfonic acid was used as an acidic hydrotrope for lignin depolymerization, followed by a conventional hydrothermal treatment. Detailed investigations indicated that the observed concentration-dependent feature for GQDs was attributed to the stack of graphene basal plane and the interaction of functional groups. Moreover, it was found that the concentration-dependent emissive GQDs were endowed with ultralong-term photostability (12 months) and negligible cytotoxicity, promising them potential applications in anti-counterfeiting and bioimaging. Overall, this study develops a feasible and sustainable strategy for opening up enormous opportunities to use renewable lignin-derived GQDs for more technological applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lignin
Graphene quantum dots
Concentration-dependent emission
Anti-counterfeiting
Bioimaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of lignin-derived graphene quantum dots (LGQDs)

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Key Laboratory of Pulp and Paper Science and Technology of Ministry of Education of China
Natural Science Foundation for Guangdong Province

National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Program for the Guangdong Introducing Innovative and Entrepreneurial Team ( 2016ZT06C412 ), the Natural Science Foundation for Guangdong Province ( 2019B151502038 ), the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 22078069 ), the Leading Innovative and Entrepreneur Team Introduction Program of Zhejiang Province ( 2017R01007 ), the Project Supported by the Foundation (No. KF201912 ) of Key Laboratory of Pulp and Paper Science and Technology of Ministry of Education of China . We also would like to thank Dr. S. Yang (Advanced Analysis & Testing Center, Nanjing Forestry University) and Dr. C. Su for providing valuable help of NMR tests and analysis.

This work was supported by the Program for the Guangdong Introducing Innovative and Entrepreneurial Team (2016ZT06C412), the Natural Science Foundation for Guangdong Province (2019B151502038), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22078069), the Leading Innovative and Entrepreneur Team Introduction Program of Zhejiang Province (2017R01007), the Project Supported by the Foundation (No. KF201912) of Key Laboratory of Pulp and Paper Science and Technology of Ministry of Education of China. We also would like to thank Dr. S. Yang (Advanced Analysis & Testing Center, Nanjing Forestry University) and Dr. C. Su for providing valuable help of NMR tests and analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A schematic diagram of the preparation of LGQDs by applying the two-step method: (1) O-acid initiated the depolymerization of AL, the former could be reused by a facile recrystallization; (2) a conventional hydrothermal carbonization. Camera images of (a) AL and (b) Eucalyptus. The supposed macromolecular structure (morphology) of (c) AL, (d) LNPs, and (e) LGQDs. (f) Camera images of LGQDs of different solutions (300, 30, 3, 0.3, 0.03 mg·L−1 from left to right labelled as 1#, 2#, 3#, 4#, 5#) and DI water (test tube 6# on the rightest side) under daylight (top) or 365 nm UV light (bottom).

(a) Typical TEM image of LGQDs with a scale bar of 20 nm (left inset: an individual nanocrystal of LGQDs shows a lattice fringe of approximately 0.21 nm, scale bar of 2 nm. Right inset: corresponding FFT pattern image, scale bar of 4 nm−1). (b) The tapping mode AFM image of LGQDs with a scale bar of 500 nm (inset: thickness (height) profile along the green line. Scale bar, 2 nm). (c) Height distribution of LGQDs calculated from (b). (d) The Raman spectra, (e) FTIR spectra, and (f) XRD patterns of AL, LNPs and LGQDs.
Two-dimensional HSQC NMR spectra of (a) AL, (b) LNPs, and (c) LGQDs, with detailed signals of side-chain region and aromatic region.

(a) UV absorbance spectra of LGQDs of different concentrations (0.03 to 3000 mg·L−1). (b) The 3D fluorescence plots of LGQDs are acquired at different excitation wavelengths from 280 to 500 nm with an increment of 20 nm. (c) PL spectra of LGQDs of different concentrations (0.03 to 3000 mg·L−1) and the 30 mg·L−1 LGQDs solutions with different glycerol contents (solid lines, 0–90 wt%), exposed under the same excitation wavelength as 380 nm. (d) Effects of glycerol content on the particle size and maximal PL intensity of the 30 mg·L−1 LGQDs solutions. (e) The Tauc plots of LGQDs solutions with different concentrations. The black lines are the tangents of the curves while the intersection value on the X-axis is the band gap (eV). (f) The time-resolved PL spectra of LGQDs solution with different concentrations (3 and 30 mg·L−1).

(a) Camera photographs of a piece of cash that is written with the number “50” using LGQDs solutions of different concentrations (10–30 mg·L−1). The written number “50” is invisible under daylight (a) unless excited by 365 nm UV light (b), of which the concentration is 30, 20, and 10 mg·L−1, respectively, from left to right. (b) Camera photographs of a commercially available special paper decorated with many dyed fibers and one bundle of them was painted by LGQDs solution (3 mg·L−1), in which the written information was invisible under daylight (left) unless excited by 365 nm UV light (right). This special paper is widely used in the packaging of luxury commodities such as high-grade tea, cosmetics, and jewelries, etc. (c) PL emission spectra of LGQDs solution (30 mg·L−1) photobleached by H2O2 (150 nM) for different time durations varied from 0 to 32 min. (d) pH-responsiveness study of LGQDs within the pH range of 3.0–13.0 in an increment of 2.0 with regard to their UV transmittance and PL emission spectra.

(a) CIE 1976 chromaticity coordinates of LGQDs upon excitation at 380 nm. (b) Cell viabilities (%) of both L929 cells and 3 T3 cells after incubation with LGQDs within the concentration range of 0–100 mg·L−1 for 48 h by CCK-8 kit. Fluorescent, bright field confocal images of LGQDs (50 mg·L−1) in rBMSCs cells, under excitation wavelengths of (c) 408 and (d) 488 nm, respectively.

head.ce:title: Concentration-dependent emissive lignin-derived graphene quantum dots for bioimaging and anti-counterfeiting

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Zhejiang Jinjiahao Green Nanomaterial Co., Ltd., Quzhou 324400, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) derived from lignin, which is the most naturally abundant aromatic biopolymer on earth, have found promising application in many fields. Here, we demonstrate that GQDs with concentration-dependent photoluminescence emission can be produced from lignin by employing a non-oxidative method. In brief, o-aminobenzenesulfonic acid was used as an acidic hydrotrope for lignin depolymerization, followed by a conventional hydrothermal treatment. Detailed investigations indicated that the observed concentration-dependent feature for GQDs was attributed to the stack of graphene basal plane and the interaction of functional groups. Moreover, it was found that the concentration-dependent emissive GQDs were endowed with ultralong-term photostability (12 months) and negligible cytotoxicity, promising them potential applications in anti-counterfeiting and bioimaging. Overall, this study develops a feasible and sustainable strategy for opening up enormous opportunities to use renewable lignin-derived GQDs for more technological applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene, one of the thinnest and the strongest known 2D materials, can exhibit giant intrinsic mobility and travel for micrometers without scattering at room temperature. As the latest addition to the graphene family, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have recently attracted growing interest from various fields. Different from graphene, GQDs are with three dimensions typically below 10 nm or smaller, thus can be regarded as a 0D nanomaterial composed of a graphene core with some functional groups or doped heteroatoms [1,2]. GQDs are usually endowed with excellent photoluminescence (PL), photostability, dispersibility, and biocompatibility. These unique merits allow them to be extensively used as ultimate components for optoelectronics [3], photodetectors [4], photocatalysts [5], biosensors [6], bioimaging [7], anti-counterfeiting [8], and so on [9,10].

Tremendous efforts have been devoted to the synthesis of highly photoluminescent GQDs. These synthetic strategies are mainly based on either “top-down” cutting from bulk carbon materials (e.g. coal, carbon black) or “bottom-up” fusing small molecules/polymers (e.g. glucose, pyrene) [11]. In comparison, usually bottom-up methods can synergistically offer relatively high yield with heteroatom(s) doping. But they may result in large heterogeneity in composition, size, or shape of GQDs, which will hinder the mass-production of them. Therefore, a successful application based on one kind of bottom-up synthesized GQDs may not be feasible to another kind. Top-down methods can utilize cheap bulk carbon materials as precursors and can be applied to any graphitized materials, which holds considerable promise in possible mass production. However, there are still issues to be addressed since they have relatively low production yield and require longer reaction time, as well as not easily disposable strong oxidants [12–15].

Ever since Albinsson et al. [16] firstly reported that lignin carried arylconjugated carbonyl groups could emit fluorescence under ultraviolet radiation in 1999, lignin has long been considered as a good candidate for optical applications [17]. In recent years, it has been frequently reported that high-performance GQDs could be fabricated from lignin. Different from the fossil fuel based coal and carbon black, lignin is the most abundant renewable source of aromatics so requires much milder treatments for GQDs production [18–21]. Based on a sonication-assisted nitric acid oxidative cutting with subsequent hydrothermal process at 180 °C, Ding et al. [7] successfully fabricated the lignin-derived GQDs (LGQDs) with excitation-dependent emission. Such method could produce single-crystalline GQDs with outstanding properties and precisely controlled size for bioimaging. By employing a similar approach, Jiang et al. [5] later synthesized GQDs from the lignin-rich black liquor of papermaking. Further compositing these GQDs with TiO2 significantly facilitated solar-powered generation of H2 from water or CO from H2O-CO2. Nevertheless, the existing methods result in uncontrollable and undesirable intermediate products of lignin and lack tailorable PL, and lignin treated in these ways may over oxidize and waste.

Here, we successfully fabricated the concentration-dependent emissive lignin-derived graphene quantum dots (LGQDs) using a non-oxidative two-step method. Alkali lignin by-produced from pulping industry was opportunely depolymerized by an acidic hydrotrope within 20 min on the basis of an ether cleavage mechanism, followed by a conventional hydrothermal carbonization to result in the final product. The chemical structure and optical properties of these LGQDs were later carefully investigated. The results demonstrated that LGQDs were endowed with concentration-dependent emissive characteristics, as well as long-term stability and good environmental tolerance. Thus, it was believed that LGQDs could be utilized as a promising invisible ink in anti-counterfeiting. Meanwhile, ultralow cytotoxicity of LGQDs was also confirmed that they could promise great potential in cellular imaging.

Alkali lignin (AL) was provided by Hunan Juntai Pulp Paper Shuntai Technology Co. Ltd. (China), which was a by-product of kraft pulping of Eucalyptus from acid separation of the resultant black liquor. All other chemicals (analytical grade) were purchased from commercial suppliers and used as received without further purification. Deionized (DI) water was used in all experiments. The dialysis tubing with cut-off molecular weight of 3500 Da and diameter of 28 mm was provided by Viskase (product number: MD44, USA). The mixed cellulose ester filter membrane (0.80 μm in pore size and 50 mm in diameter) was supplied by Shanghai Xinya Purification Equipment Co., China.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of lignin-derived graphene quantum dots (LGQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: LGQDs were obtained by a facile two-step method as reported in our previous work [6]. Briefly, alkali lignin (AL, 2.0 g) was added to a 5 wt% o-aminobenzenesulfonic acid (O-acid, C6H7NO3S) solution (1:20, w/v) under vigorous agitating at 80 °C for 20 min. After the reaction was completed, the mixture was filtered immediately to remove unreacted AL. Subsequently, the obtained filtrate was air cooled to room temperature and filtered again to remove O-acid (as recycled O-acid after further air-drying at 60 °C overnight, details shown in Table S1). The second-time filtrate was dialyzed against deionized water with daily replacement for at least 3 days, thus lignin nanoparticles (LNPs) were obtained in the resultant orange solution after dialysis.

NaOH (0.5 g) was added to the LNPs solution and sonicated for 120 min, the mixture was then transferred to a poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave (100 mL) and heated at 200 °C for 12 h. After air cooled to room temperature, the resulting black suspension was filtered to remove insoluble impurities. At last, the filtrate containing water-soluble LGQDs was dialyzed against deionized water with daily replacement for at least 3 days.

The morphology of LGQDs samples was observed using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM). An aliquot LGQDs sample (3 mg·L−1) was carefully deposited on a 300-mesh copper grid coated with carbon film or a newly peeled mica sheet, then air dried overnight. All TEM images were acquired on a JEM-2100 UHR TEM (JEOL, Japan) operated at 200 kV. The average particle size of LGQDs was calculated using the Image-Pro Plus software by randomly selecting at least 100 particles in the TEM image. AFM analysis of LGQDs samples was performed in tapping mode with a Dimension FastScan AFM (Bruker Corp., USA).

The Raman spectra collected in this study were applied using a LabRAM HR Evolution spectrometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon, France) equipped with a 532 nm laser. The Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectra were investigated on a Vertex 80 V FTIR spectrometer (Bruker Corp., Germany). The X-ray Diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained with a Bruker D8 Venture using Cu Kα radiation (60 kV, 80 mA, λ = 1.5418 Å). The transmission/absorbance spectra of LGQDs were measured using a UV759CRT UV–vis spectrophotometer within the wavelength range from 200 to 900 nm (Shanghai Youke Instrument Co. Ltd., China). The Photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the 30 mg·L−1 LGQDs solutions were recorded on a LabRAM HR Evolution spectrometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon, France), with the excitation light wavelengths from 280 to 500 nm in 20 nm increments. The quantum yield (QY) of LGQDs was calculated via a relative method using quinine sulfate in 1 M sulfuric acid as a reference [23]. The time-resolved PL spectrum of LGQDs was determined by using a FLS1000 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments., UK). The particle size distribution of LGQDs was measured by using a Zetasizer Nano ZSE instrument (Malvern Analytical., UK). The two-dimensional 1H 13C heteronuclear single-quantum coherence correlation (HSQC) nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectra of AL (dimethyl sulfoxide‑d 6), LNPs (D2O) and LGQDs (dimethyl sulfoxide‑d 6-pyridine‑d 5, 4:1 (v/v)) were recorded on an AVANCE III HD NMR spectrometer (600 MHz, Bruker Corp., Switzerland).

The identification of degradation products contained in the spent liquor of LGQDs after the hydrothermal treatment were conducted by a gas chromatography–mass spectrometry (GC–MS) technique [24]. This analysis was performed using a Trace ISQ 1300 instrument (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). A DB-5MS fused silica capillary column (30 m × 0.25 mm × 0.25 μm, J&W scientific, USA) was employed for product separation with the following temperature history: temperature holding at 60 °C for 2 min, heating to 180 °C at a rate of 10 °C/min, then heating to 250 °C at a rate of 5 °C/min and holding at 250 °C for 1 min. The high-purity helium flow rate was 1.0 mL/min. The MS was operated in electron ionization (EI) mode with the electron energy set at 70 eV. Transmission line temperature and ion source temperature were both set at 250 °C. All MS spectra were acquired using the full-scan monitoring mode, with a mass scan rang of m/z 33–450 and a solvent delay time of 2 min.

The cytotoxicity of LGQDs was evaluated in vitro by employing the mouse fibroblast cell lines L929 and 3T3 with the Cell-Counting Kit-8 assay (CCK-8) as reported by Chen et al. [25] In brief, the cells were seeded onto 96-well plates (7 × 103 cells/well) and incubated at 37 °C for 8 h. Then, the cell medium was replaced with fresh medium containing various concentrations of LGQDs and incubated at 37 °C for 72 h. After the incubation, all the cells were washed thoroughly with phosphate buffer solution (PBS, 0.01 M) and then incubated in 100 μL of Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium containing 10 μL of CCK-8 (5 mg·mL−1 in PBS) for 4 h. The absorbance in each well was measured using a microplate reader at a wavelength of 450 nm, to calculate the number of viable cells. All present cell viability data were obtained from the average of three measurements.

For the bioimaging tests, rat bone marrow stromal cells (rBMSCs) were seeded in each well and incubated at room temperature for 24 h. Next, the culture medium was removed and the cells were washed thoroughly three times (5 min each time) with PBS (0.01 M), subsequently they were fixed using 0.5 mL of a fresh 4 wt% paraformaldehyde for 30 min. After fixing, the cells were washed thoroughly three times with PBS and then rinsed in the mixture of (1:1 w/w) 6 wt% bull serum albumin and 0.02 wt% Triton X-100 in PBS at room temperature for 60 min. Followed the mixture was removed and the cells were washed thoroughly three times with PBS. Then, 200 μL of 67 mg·L−1 LGQDs solution were added to each well to incubate at room temperature under light-free conditions for 30 min. LGQDs solution was subsequently removed and the cells were washed thoroughly three times with PBS. Finally, the cells were rinsed in PBS for the observation under a Nikon A1 laser confocal total internal reflection fluorescence microscopy (Nikon Corp., Japan).

In this study, AL is employed as the feedstock is because it accounts for more than 85% of industrial lignin that is generally regarded as a by-product of limited value [26]. Fig. 1 illustrates a schematic diagram of the two-step method to produce LGQDs from AL, which is collected from the black liquor of kraft pulping of Eucalyptus that contains many aromatics [27]. During the processing, O-acid was firstly used as the acidic hydrotrope to depolymerize AL and caused a significant decrease of weight-average molecular weight (Mw) from 4300 of AL to 1797 to form LNPs (details are listed in Table S2) [28]. Upon subsequent hydrothermal treatment, aromatic rings in LNPs are fused while dehydrogenizing and deoxygenizing, rendering LGQDs with a polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons structure and as low Mw as 789 [29]. Specifically, it is found that a dilute solution of LGQDs (3 mg·L−1), which has a similar appearance as DI water under daylight, can emit green fluorescence when exposed to a 365 nm UV (3 in Fig. 1f). In other words, LGQDs disperse in a liquid medium can present a UV-switchable PL (on/off: UV/daylight) that is naked eye observable. Also, a concentration-dependent PL difference is observed that is addressed in detail later. Additionally, it is found that the used O-acid can be easily recycled by re-crystallizing at room temperature and a high total recyclability over 85.18 wt% is obtained after five consecutive cycles (Table S1).

TEM and AFM characterizations were performed to assess the morphology LGQDs. As shown in Fig. 2a, the average lateral size of LGQDs is estimated to be 2.20 ± 0.40 nm (calculated using the Image-Pro Plus software). The high-resolution TEM image (the inset of Fig. 2a) reveals that LGQDs are composed of nanocrystals with a spacing of approximately 0.21 nm, in larger consistent with that of (100) planes for graphene. Corresponding sixfold symmetrical fast Fourier transform (FFT) pattern image suggests that LGQDs are composed of hexagonal graphene-like single-crystals, instead of fullerene-like single-crystals with many five- and seven-membered rings, thanks to the relatively mild O-acid-induced transformation of AL. Besides, Fig. 2b–c display that the height of LGQDs basically ranges from 1 to 2 nm with an average of 1.64 ± 0.17 nm, which is thinner than five layers of graphene whose theoretical thickness is 0.34 nm. The above results demonstrate that LGQDs possess the 0D characteristics analogous to GQDs as previously reported, thus confirming the success of the method used here [30,31]. As shown in Fig. 2d, all of AL, LNPs and LGQDs have prominent D band and G band, while the I
D
/I
G value of LGQDs (0.98) is over that of AL (0.88). This I
D /I
G value change is commonly seen in the fabrication of graphene from carbonaceous starting materials [32]. Fig. 2e displays the FTIR transmittance spectra of AL, LNPs and LGQDs, in which the characteristic peaks of lignin are generally observed. Comparing the spectrum of AL with that of LNPs: (1) the bands assigned to CH stretching (2948 and 2843 cm−1), unconjugated C=Ostretching(1705 cm−1) and CO of the guaiacyl ring or CO stretch (1269 and 1215 cm−1) get weaker, while that assigned to ether vibrations and β-O-4 around 1035 cm−1 shrinks to a sharp peak [33]; (2) brand new peaks related to aromatic CN vibration (around 1600 cm−1) and S=Ostretching(1419 cm−1) appear [34,35]. Meanwhile, LGQDs present much alike spectrum as LNPs, except for the absent unconjugated C=Ostretchingpeak at 1705 cm−1 and the strengthened aromaticC-Hdeformation at 1120 cm−1 [36]. The XRD patterns of AL, LNPs and LGQDs are presented in Fig. 2f. AL and LGQDs have the broad peak around 20.3° (d-spacing = 0.438 nm) and 24.6° (d-spacing = 0.334 nm), respectively. Note, the d-spacing of 0.360 nm for LGQDs is quite close to that of graphene (0.334 nm), also confirming the graphene-like material formation of LGQDs here [37].

According to the above discussion, the formation of LGQDs can be tentatively proposed as follows. First, AL underwent the ether cleavage with synchronous N, S doping to form LNPs in a short period, which is well supported by the differences between the FTIR spectra LNPs and AL (Fig. 2e). Second, possible nucleophilic substitution and dehydration reaction of amine moieties with oxygen-functional groups on LNPs occurred during the hydrothermal treatment [3], resulting in the formation of LGQDs. Two-dimensional HSQC NMR spectra of AL, LNPs, and LGQDs are displayed in Fig. 3 . In contrast to AL, LNPs exhibit no signal of the condensed S/G subunit but significantly increased signals of S subunit and side-chain region, while the signals assigned to the S/G subunits of native lignin are almost absent in LGQDs except for the methoxyl related signal (OMe). These results reveals that the condensed S subunit of AL was depolymerized by O-acid to form LNPs to expose much more signals related to the side-chain region, and further hydrothermal treatment transformed LNPs into LGQDs with very few characteristic lignin structures. This mechanism is also evidenced by GC–MS analysis as there are many lignin dimers detected in the spent liquor of LGQDs (Fig. S1). This is in good consistent with that reported by Pan et al. [2].

The optical properties of LGQDs are investigated by UV absorbance and PL emission spectra. As exhibited in Fig. 4a, LGQDs have two prominent peaks centered at 240 nm and 280 nm that are assigned to the π–π* transition of lignin-derived sp2 aromatic domains and n–π* transition between energy levels from the surface states arising from the functional groups, respectively [7,15]. LGQDs also display a broad red-shifted absorption tail in the entire visible region, the intensity of which is significantly increased as the concentration increases. Besides, the 3D fluorescence plots of LGQDs are quantified and depicted in 3D-Measurements, which suggest an intriguing excitation-dependent emission within the measured excitation wavelengths from 280 to 500 nm (Fig. 4b). Moreover, it is found that LGQDs possess obvious concentration-dependent PL emissive feature, as shown in Fig. 4c. Although the dilute solution of LGQDs (0.03 mg·L−1) is almost non-emissive, continuously increasing the concentration clearly results in a sustained increase of PL intensity. The maximal PL intensity of LGQDs (30 mg·L−1) appears at 484 nm when excited with 380 nm, along with a high QY of 20.63% may be attributed to the doping effect as carefully addressed in the following. This QY is comparative to most reported lignin-derived GQDs (Table S3). Beyond 30 mg·L−1, sharply decreased PL intensity is observed for LGQDs that is usually attributed to the aggregation-caused quenching [38–40]. In addition to these, Fig. S2 verifies the colloidal nature of the 30 mg·L−1 LGQDs solution as it presents an apparent Tyndall phenomenon. In this case, a desired amount of highly viscous glycerol is added in to produce the 30 mg·L−1 LGQDs solution as a poor solvent. As expected, Fig. 4b clearly shows that the PL intensity is continuously increases as the glycerol content grows from 0 wt% to 90 wt%, and higher glycerol content can result in even higher PL intensity enhancement. The enhancement of QY/PL intensity may be attributed to the existence of various fluorescent centers and enhanced aggregation of LGQDs under higher concentration. It was carefully addressed in our previous study that LGQDs were endowed with multiple PL sources [6], while more evidence is needed to corroborate the aggregation of LGQDs. To the best of our knowledge, the aggregation of GQDs should be related to the decrease in the solvation of the hydrophilic functional groups [38]. If so, the non-radiative decay of LGQDs would be reduced as the concentration increases until it meets the limit, which contrarily enhances the non-radiative relaxation and results in lower PL intensities. Meanwhile, since there is no obvious red-shift of the PL emission, it is possible that higher concentration of LGQDs may induce continued aggregation in horizontal dimension, finally enlarging the graphene basal plane to have smaller bandgaps than that of a single LGQDs [41].

The above hypothesis is confirmed by the DLS measurement and Tauc plots evaluation. As exhibited in Fig. 4d, there is a good correlation between glycerol content and particle size (R
2 = 0.9869), as well as between the latter and the maximal PL intensity (R
2 = 0.9795). On the other hand, the Tauc plots, (αhν)2 versus hν, which can be converted from the UV absorption spectrum and usually used to estimate the optical band gaps (E
g ) of GQDs, in which α, h, and ν are the absorption coefficient, Planck constant, and light frequency, respectively [42]. As shown in Fig. 4e, the E
g values of LGQDs significantly decrease from 6.29, 5.85, 4.73, to 3.43 eV with increasing concentration. By taking account of their colloidal nature, increasing the concentration of LGQDs leads to the increased aggregation, during which usually occurs the energy level splitting and broadening and consequently the reduction in the band gap (E
g ) [11]. In addition, the time resolved PL spectra of LGQDs solution with different concentrations were determined and displayed in Fig. 4f. The 30 mg·L−1 LGQDs solution has longer lifetime (19.13 ns) than that of the 3 mg·L−1 LGQDs solution (16.30 ns). Both solutions exhibit biexponential fluorescence decays composed of one short nanosecond term, τ
1 , and a longer term, τ
2 , which have previously been attributed to monomer and excimer fluorescence lifetimes, respectively [43]. Apparently, with the increase in concentration, LGQDs tended to aggregate considering their colloidal nature, thus more nonradiative pathways would be blocked to primarily let carrier transfer through the radiative channels. In other words, more energy included would be transferred and then emitted rather than dissipated by nonradiative transitions. Consequently, higher QY and longer lifetime can be achieved [42,44]. This is also supported by the QY measurement displayed in Fig. S3 as the solution of 30 mg·L−1 has higher QY (20.63%) than that of the solution of 3 mg·L−1 (11.84%). In summary, we can conclude that the concentration-dependent PL emission of LGQDs is dominated by the self-aggregation process [15,45].

By taking all these advantages into account, LGQDs may be widely applicable in many fields. Particularly, since the down-conversion (the maximal PL intensity appears at 488 nm when excited with 380 nm, Fig. 4c) and concentration-dependent PL emissions are clearly noticed, it is believed that LGQDs hold great promise for potential applications in anti-counterfeiting and bioimaging.

In order to demonstrate the practical application of LGQDs as the anti-counterfeiting material, different kinds of paper were used here as the writable matrices to analysis their performance. As shown in Fig. 5a, a Chinese currency of 50 Yuan encrypted by different LGQDs solutions presents clearly concentration-dependent traces. Fig. 5b displays a commercially available special paper for packaging, in which the fiber bundles can form highly unrepeatable patterns to offer planar anti-counterfeiting. By randomly painting one fiber bundle with the 30 mg·L−1 LGQDs solution, the painted fiber bundle is invisible under daylight (graph i) unless pumping with a 365 nm UV lamp (graph ii). On the other hand, the long-term stability of LGQDs was also evaluated. As shown in Fig. S4, an ordinary filter paper loaded the 30 mg·L−1 LGQDs preserves the same appearance very similar to blank one unless exposed to a 365 nm UV. After placing the paper in a non-air-conditioned room, its UV-switchability could be remained for up to 12 months, thus confirming LGQDs with good stability and weather resistance. Moreover, when mixing LGQDs with PL quenchable H2O2 (150 nM) or pH- tunable acid/base, negligible decrease (≤3%) of PL intensity was observed within the tested 32 min, while the wide pH ranges from 3.0 to 13.0 only enables a PL intensity change up to 26% for LGQDs, as exhibited in Fig. 5c and d (see more details in Fig. S5). For this outstanding photostability of LGQDs, many literatures reported that long chain hydrocarbon is indispensable for preparing stable colloidal quantum dots [23,46]. Obviously, there is no long chain in O-acid or rather sulfonic group o-substituted aniline that makes it a potential conjugated amine. Besides, the polar sulfonic group and amine group can endow LGQDs with improved π-π* conjugated structure to avoid being influenced by the varied temperature, relative humidity, oxidants, and acids/bases. The electrochemically inaccessible nature of lignin backbone may also contribute to the relative pH-stable pattern of LGQDs [47].

Under 380 nm UV irradiation, the coordinate on the Commission Internationale de L'Eclairage (CIE) 1976 chromaticity appears at (0.06, 0.52) for LGQDs, as shown in Fig. 6a. Moreover, the full-color emission phenomenon is readily observed for LGQDs, as well as the limited cytotoxic effect with high cell viabilities above 90% (Fig. S6 and Fig. 6b). Therefore, LGQDs are suggested to be the potential candidate for multi-color PL bioimaging. Subsequently, we performed in vitro bioimaging of rBMSCs treated with 50 mg·L−1 LGQDs as the PL nanomarker with a Laser scanning confocal microscopy. It is clear that LGQDs internalized by rBMSCs can emit fluorescence include strong blue light and green light (Fig. 6c–d) at excitation wavelengths of 408 and 488 nm, respectively. LGQDs shows good selectivity as the extracellular matrix maintains in dark while the cytoplasm is lit up. Significantly, most nuclei with clear outlines can be easily observed that suggests LGQDs a good PL label for bioimaging (highlighted by the circled dash line in Fig. 6c–d).

Concentration-dependent emissive LGQDs are successfully prepared through a facile two-step method includes acidic hydrotrope and hydrothermal treatment. This intriguing characteristic emission is due to the stack of graphene basal plane and the interaction of functional groups as the concentration increased, which led to smaller bandgaps and longer PL lifetime. Subsequent experiments attest that LGQDs can be used as the invisible ink to load writable information with inconspicuous PL fading (longer than 12 months) and great weather resistance, which is promising to serve as a good complement to the current anti-counterfeiting technologies. Besides, the potential utilization of LGQDs in vitro cellular imaging through endocytosis is also confirmed, owing to their ultralow cytotoxicity and good selectivity that have limited effect on the cells. We believe this study will additionally evidence that renewable lignin can be an everlasting source of high-performance GQDs for wider applications.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.
CRediT authorship contribution statement

Ruibin Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Investigation, Writing- Original draft preparation, Writing- Reviewing and Editing. Ziyu Guo: Investigation. Yuqian Liu: Investigation. Liang Jiao: Investigation. Tianhua Xiao: Investigation. Hairui Ji: Investigation and Funding acquisition. Feiguo Hua: Investigation and Funding acquisition. Yanlin Qin: Supervision, Writing- original draft and Funding acquisition. Hongqi Dai: Methodology, Supervision and Funding acquisition. Yonggang Min: Supervision and Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Program for the Guangdong Introducing Innovative and Entrepreneurial Team (2016ZT06C412), the Natural Science Foundation for Guangdong Province (2019B151502038), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22078069), the Leading Innovative and Entrepreneur Team Introduction Program of Zhejiang Province (2017R01007), the Project Supported by the Foundation (No. KF201912) of Key Laboratory of Pulp and Paper Science and Technology of Ministry of Education of China. We also would like to thank Dr. S. Yang (Advanced Analysis & Testing Center, Nanjing Forestry University) and Dr. C. Su for providing valuable help of NMR tests and analysis.
